title: CNN This Morning · 2026-06-10 · 缅因州参议员选举：普拉特纳 vs. 柯林斯
summary: Trong cuộc bầu cử sơ bộ của đảng Dân chủ tại Maine, Graham Prattner, người vướng nhiều bê bối, đã đánh bại Thống đốc Janet Mills với khoảng 75% phiếu bầu, và sẽ thách thức Thượng nghị sĩ đảng Cộng hòa Susan Collins. Prattner cần tranh thủ sự ủng hộ của những người ủng hộ Mills, trong khi Collins vẫn có lợi thế trong số các cử tri độc lập. Tin tức khác: Mỹ tiến hành tấn công tên lửa vào Iran; OpenAI nộp đơn IPO; Phiên điều trần hồ sơ Epstein; Phát ngôn gây tranh cãi của ứng cử viên thống đốc Colorado Victor Marx.

paragraphs.speaker: Audie Cornish
paragraphs.en: Today in the group chat, Democrats in Maine vote to stick with Graham Platner. Could that cost the party a shot at flipping the Senate this fall? REP. ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY): If the choice on the ballot is between that and a senator who's voted to take health care away from millions of Americans, that's the situation that we have to weigh.
paragraphs.cn: Hôm nay trong cuộc trò chuyện nhóm, các đảng viên Dân chủ Maine đã bỏ phiếu cho Graham Prattner. Liệu điều này có khiến họ mất cơ hội lật ngược Thượng viện vào mùa thu này không? Hạ nghị sĩ Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (Dân chủ New York): Nếu lựa chọn trên lá phiếu là giữa anh ta và một thượng nghị sĩ đã bỏ phiếu tước quyền chăm sóc sức khỏe của hàng triệu người Mỹ, thì đó là tình huống chúng ta phải cân nhắc.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Missiles light up the sky in the Middle East again. Will the strained ceasefire last long enough for the U.S. to deliver that long- promised deal with Iran? And a big day for the Epstein files. What will Bill Gates tell lawmakers about his links to the late convicted sex offender.
paragraphs.cn: Tên lửa lại thắp sáng bầu trời Trung Đông. Liệu lệnh ngừng bắn mong manh có thể kéo dài đủ lâu để Mỹ thực hiện thỏa thuận cam kết dài hạn với Iran? Hồ sơ Epstein bước vào ngày quan trọng. Bill Gates sẽ nói gì với các nhà lập pháp về mối liên hệ của ông với tội phạm tình dục đã bị kết án và đã qua đời?

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: Do you think that you've killed people as an adult? VICTOR MARX (R),
paragraphs.cn: Bạn có nghĩ rằng bạn đã từng giết người khi trưởng thành không? Victor Marx (Cộng hòa),

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: This is a candidate who also claims he killed someone as a child. Meet the frontrunner in the Republican race for governor of Colorado. And it's the $3.5 trillion question on Wall Street. Has the A.I. boom gotten ahead of itself?
paragraphs.cn: Đây là một ứng cử viên tự nhận đã giết người khi còn nhỏ. Hãy gặp người dẫn đầu cuộc bầu cử sơ bộ của đảng Cộng hòa cho chức thống đốc Colorado. Đây là câu hỏi trị giá 3,5 nghìn tỷ đô la của Phố Wall. Liệu cơn sốt AI đã đi trước thời đại?

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: OpenAI and Anthropic plan to have IPOs at release before the end of this year, signaling to me that the A.I. bubble is about to pop. GRAHAM PLATNER (D),
paragraphs.cn: OpenAI và Anthropic có kế hoạch phát hành cổ phiếu lần đầu ra công chúng vào cuối năm nay, điều này cho tôi thấy bong bóng AI sắp vỡ. Graham Prattner (Dân chủ),

paragraphs.speaker: Maine Senate Candidate
paragraphs.en: To any of those who feel let down or disappointed or disillusioned, it is my job to earn your trust, faith, and support. And I will spend every day of this campaign. And if I have the privilege every day in the United States Senate doing exactly that.
paragraphs.cn: Đối với bất kỳ ai cảm thấy thất vọng hay ảo tưởng, công việc của tôi là giành được lòng tin, niềm tin và sự ủng hộ của các bạn. Tôi sẽ dành từng ngày trong chiến dịch này. Và nếu tôi có vinh dự được phục vụ tại Thượng viện Hoa Kỳ, tôi sẽ làm như vậy mỗi ngày.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Graham Platner punches his ticket. But now he's got to convince voters to choose him over a five-term senator. Good morning everybody. I'm Audie Cornish. Here is where we begin. That big statement out of Maine. Democrat Graham Platner, despite all the scandals, earned about three- fourths of primary voters' support over Governor Janet Mills. Moments after clinching the nomination, he turned his attention to his new opponent, Republican Senator Susan Collins.
paragraphs.cn: Graham Prattner đã giành được đề cử. Nhưng giờ anh ấy phải thuyết phục cử tri chọn anh ấy thay vì một thượng nghị sĩ đã phục vụ năm nhiệm kỳ. Chào buổi sáng mọi người. Tôi là Audie Cornish. Chúng ta bắt đầu. Một tuyên bố quan trọng từ Maine. Graham Prattner, đảng viên Dân chủ, mặc dù vướng nhiều bê bối, đã giành được khoảng ba phần tư số phiếu bầu sơ bộ, đánh bại Thống đốc Janet Mills. Ngay sau khi nhận được đề cử, anh ấy đã chuyển sự chú ý sang đối thủ mới của mình, Thượng nghị sĩ đảng Cộng hòa Susan Collins.

paragraphs.speaker: Platner
paragraphs.en: Susan Collins may have started -- may have started her career decades ago in Washington with good intentions, but she has become just as spineless and corrupt as the establishment she now serves. If you are an independent voice, why do you vote with Donald Trump 95 percent of the time?
paragraphs.cn: Susan Collins có thể đã bắt đầu sự nghiệp của mình ở Washington với những ý định tốt cách đây nhiều thập kỷ, nhưng bà ấy đã trở nên thiếu xương sống và tham nhũng như chính quyền mà bà ấy phục vụ. Nếu bạn là một tiếng nói độc lập, tại sao bạn lại bỏ phiếu ủng hộ Donald Trump 95% thời gian?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Platner has a lot of work to do to get Mills voters on his side.
paragraphs.cn: Prattner cần làm rất nhiều việc để những người ủng hộ Mills đứng về phía anh ấy.

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Female
paragraphs.en: I couldn't honestly support Platner, even though some of the things that he says are really good.
paragraphs.cn: Tôi thực sự không thể ủng hộ Prattner, mặc dù anh ấy nói một số điều khá hay.

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: I don't think he's ready to be a senator. I'm leaning towards Collins unless the guy shows something else, but I just -- he's not there yet. And --
paragraphs.cn: Tôi không nghĩ anh ấy đã sẵn sàng làm thượng nghị sĩ. Tôi có xu hướng ủng hộ Collins, trừ khi anh chàng đó thể hiện điều gì khác, nhưng tôi chỉ -- -- anh ấy chưa đạt đến trình độ đó. Và -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Female
paragraphs.en: And so, what will you do in November if its Platner versus Collins?
paragraphs.cn: So, if November is Plattner vs. Collins, what would you do?

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Female
paragraphs.en: That's a really good question. I'm a Democrat, but I voted for Susan Collins several times, because I think that she does care about the state of Maine. But it's going to be a real problem for me in November.
paragraphs.cn: That's a great question. I'm a Democrat, but I've voted for Susan Collins many times because I think she really cares about Maine. But November will be a real problem for me.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, today in the group chat, Mike Leon, host of the "Can We Please Talk" podcast; Jared Stepman, columnist with "The Daily Signal"; and Max Rose, former Democratic congressman from New York. And Max, I'm actually going to start with you, because you're with VoteVets, which means you guys had a dog in the fight in this race. You guys have been giving a lot of money across the country. And what is it? Let me just show what -- what's at stake here. Democrats' chances of flipping the Maine Senate seat.
paragraphs.cn: So, today in the group chat, we have Mike Leone, host of the podcast 'Can We Talk', Jared Steppman, columnist for 'The Daily Signal', and former New York Democratic Congressman Max Rose. Max, I actually want to start with you because you work at VoteVets, which means you also have a stake in this race. You've invested heavily nationwide. So what's the situation? Let me show you -- -- here's the key. The Democratic chance to flip the Maine Senate seat.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: It was pretty good. Maybe this has jeopardized it. We're going to show people a chart while you're talking so you can see what's at stake. Is this race as close as you all hoped it would be, now that Graham has struggled with these scandals? MAX ROSE (D),
paragraphs.cn: It was pretty good. Maybe it's been jeopardized. We'll show people a chart as you speak so they can see the stakes. Since Graham is struggling with these scandals, is this race as close as you all hoped? Max Rose (Democrat):

paragraphs.speaker: Former New York Congressman
paragraphs.en: The race is always going to be close. Because --
paragraphs.cn: This race was always going to be close. Because -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Not with a five-term senator, it's not. A lot of times it can be a blowout, right? Like what -- how do you look at what he needs to accomplish?
paragraphs.cn: For a five-term senator, not really. A lot of times it could be a landslide, right? Like -- -- what do you think he needs to accomplish?

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.en: Well, I think that we're saying the same thing --
paragraphs.cn: Well, I think we're saying the same thing -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.en: -- in essence, right? Susan Collins is tough to beat. What I would say about the Susan Collins race is that we've seen in the past, is you're not going to win this seat by electing a Susan Collins light or putting forward a Susan Collins light in a race. You are going to beat her by energizing your base, particularly in a midterm election.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- essentially, right? Susan Collins is hard to beat. What I want to say about Susan Collins's campaign is that in the past we've seen that you can't win this seat by electing a 'Susan Collins lite' or putting a 'Susan Collins lite' in the race. You beat her by energizing your base, especially in a midterm.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.en: There is no doubt, though, that this election has got to be a referendum on Susan Collins: on the ways in which she has voted to empower Donald Trump; on her refusal to hold Donald Trump accountable; on the ways in which she has said, I might be fair, but I want to take healthcare away from millions of people. Says the right thing often; does the wrong thing very frequently.
paragraphs.cn: But there's no doubt that this election must be a referendum on Susan Collins: on the way she voted to empower Donald Trump; on the way she refused to hold Donald Trump accountable; on the way she said, I may be fair, but I want to take healthcare away from millions of people. She often says the right things; very frequently does the wrong things.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. So, this is very much the message we hear over and over again. And I love that Platner in front of him, it said, "They don't know Maine." You know what I mean? I want to play two clips back-to-back, because it sort of, I think, represents something of this race. First, I'm going to play for you J.D. Vance and how he talks about Susan Collins.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. So, that's exactly the message we keep hearing. I like the sign in front of him that says 'They don't know Maine'. You know what I mean? First, I'll play a clip of J.D. Vance talking about Susan Collins.

paragraphs.en: And then I'm going to play AOC and how she's talking about Platner. You tell me how you hear the tone of these. J.D. VANCE (R),
paragraphs.cn: Then I'll play a clip of AOC talking about Plattner. You tell me how you feel about these tones. J.D. Vance (Republican):

paragraphs.speaker: U.S. Vice President
paragraphs.en: Here's the thing I'll say about Susan Collins, is sometimes I get frustrated with Susan Collins. I almost wish that she was more partisan. But the thing I love about Susan is she is independent, because Maine is an independent state. And frankly, if she was as partisan as I sometimes wish that she was, she would not be a good fit for the people of Maine. So, let's give a shout-out to Susan Collins, who's doing a great job.
paragraphs.cn: What I'll say about Susan Collins is that sometimes I get frustrated with her. I almost wish she were more partisan. But what I like about Susan is that she's independent, because Maine is an independent state. Frankly, if she were as partisan as I sometimes wish, she wouldn't be a good fit for the people of Maine. So, let's give a shout-out to Susan Collins, she's doing a great job.

paragraphs.speaker: Ocasio-Cortez
paragraphs.en: When it comes to the substance of this reporting, obviously, there's a lot in that behavior that's really challenging. It's hard to stomach, you know, in some of it. But -- but at the end of the day, I think that this is a choice. If that choice on the ballot is between that and a senator who's voted to take healthcare away from millions of Americans, that's the situation that we have to weigh.
paragraphs.cn: As for the substance of this report, clearly there is a lot that is very disturbing in that behavior. Some of it is hard to accept. But ultimately, I think it's a choice. If the choice on the ballot is between that and a senator who voted to strip millions of Americans of healthcare, that's the situation we have to weigh.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I played these, because one is like, it's hard to stomach. And the other is like, I get frustrated. Shout-out though, anyway, because I guess Maine. And I was, like, these are not ringing endorsements. MIKE LEON, HOST, "CAN WE PLEASE TALK?"
paragraphs.cn: I played these because one is hard to accept and the other frustrates me. But still, kudos because I think it's Maine. I thought, these are not enthusiastic endorsements. Mike Leon, host, "Can we talk about it?"

paragraphs.speaker: Podcast
paragraphs.en: But Audie, not only are they not ringing endorsements, the voter that we played in the package before listen to her tone. Maine independents are the second largest party in Maine.
paragraphs.cn: But Audi, not only is it not an enthusiastic endorsement, listen to the tone of that voter we played in the earlier clip. Independents in Maine are the second largest party in the state.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. And a woman, in particular.
paragraphs.cn: Yes, especially women.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.en: And a woman and a Democrat who said she voted for Susan Collins, against her will, it sounded like, from that thing. So -- but those are -- those are some of the things that we're going to have to consider now. It's really about the character quality is what something that Graham Platner has been trying to say: Forget about that. I'm from here. It's how she's voted, to Max's point.
paragraphs.cn: A female Democrat who said she voted for Susan Collins but sounded like she was doing it against her will. So -- -- but these are some of the things we have to consider now. It's really about character traits, which is what Graham Plattner has been trying to say: forget that. I'm from here. What matters is her voting record, as Max said.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.en: But then also you hear the voters, and they're, like, a little, still unsure because of those character issues with Platner. So, it's going to be really interesting.
paragraphs.cn: But you also hear voters, they're still a bit uncertain because of Plattner's character issues. So, it will be very interesting.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And he's been telling a story for a while of, like, you know, I was -- went through difficult times. Now I'm a person who has changed. I want to ask one more thing. Collins and Trump, that's not a tight bond. OK? Can I just put it that way? Here's a list I'm going to play for you. Just some moments over the past couple of months where you hear her talking around Trump policies and where she stands on them. SEN. SUSAN COLLINS (R-ME): The 60 days is a trigger.
paragraphs.cn: And he's been telling a story, like, you know, I've been through hard times. Now I'm a changed person. I want to ask one more thing. Collins and Trump, not close. Okay? Can I say that? Senator Susan Collins (R-ME): 60 days is a trigger point.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: After 60 days, in my view, the president has to obtain congressional approval or Congress can block it. I do not believe that individuals who were convicted of violence against police officers on January 6th should be entitled to reimbursement for their legal fees. Concerned about the cuts in Medicaid and the impact on my state, but other states, as well. I've talked with the secretary of homeland security.
paragraphs.cn: After 60 days, in my view, the president must get congressional approval, or Congress can block it. I don't think people convicted of violently assaulting police on January 6 should be entitled to reimbursement for legal fees. I'm concerned about the impact of Medicaid cuts on my state and others. I've spoken with the Secretary of Homeland Security.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I have strongly recommended to her that there be a pause on the enforcement surge in both Maine and in Minnesota.
paragraphs.cn: I strongly advised her to suspend enforcement actions in Maine and Minnesota.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Now, I've chased her in the hallways before, too. She often ends up voting with Republicans, whatever that party line is. But is Trump going to get involved in this simply because of what's at stake with the Senate? Or is he going to send surrogates? He'll be like, OK, J.D., you go there? Like, can she expect any support there? JARRETT STEPMAN, COLUMNIST, "THE DAILY SIGNAL": Yes, I feel that's why he sent J.D. and I feel he will, if he's smart, actually keep some distance from this.
paragraphs.cn: I've chased her down the hallway before. She often ends up voting with Republicans, regardless of party lines. But will Trump get involved just because of the Senate stakes? Or will he send surrogates? Will he say, okay, J.D., you go there? Can she count on support? Jarrett Stepman, Daily Signal columnist: Yes, I think that's why he sent J.D., and I think if he's smart, he'll actually keep some distance.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I mean part of the -- the thing at stake here is that they're independent. Maine is a little bit different. I mean, both these candidates come from sort of -- almost independent of the party.
paragraphs.cn: I mean, part of the stakes is that they are independent. Maine is a bit different. I mean, both candidates come from a kind of -- -- almost independent of party.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.en: Because they -- they really focus on the issues related to Maine.
paragraphs.cn: Because they really focus on issues relevant to Maine.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: We should say, Platner is recruiting from outside the party. Yes.
paragraphs.cn: Chúng ta nên nói rằng, Plattner đang tuyển người từ bên ngoài đảng. Đúng vậy.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.en: Exactly. So, they're both sort of weirdly outsiders, even though Collins has been Republican part of the party for a long time, she's always struck an independent game this entire time. So, I think that's going to continue during this race. That is her path. Because if she does get too close to Trump, that's going to be the argument saying, whoa, she's too MAGA. She's not -- too much for me, which is more or less a blue state.
paragraphs.cn: Chính xác. Vì vậy, cả hai đều hơi kỳ lạ khi là người ngoài cuộc, mặc dù Collins từ lâu đã là một phần của Đảng Cộng hòa, nhưng bà vẫn giữ thái độ độc lập. Vì vậy, tôi nghĩ tình trạng này sẽ tiếp tục trong chiến dịch này. Đó là con đường của bà ấy. Bởi vì nếu bà ấy quá thân thiết với Trump, sẽ có người nói, ồ, bà ấy quá MAGA. Bà ấy không phù hợp với tôi, và về cơ bản đây là một bang xanh.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, you're saying there's nothing to be gained there of him coming.
paragraphs.cn: Vậy, ý anh là anh ta chẳng có lợi ích gì khi đến.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK, you guys stay with me, because we're going to talk about a couple other states this hour, as well. We're also still waiting for news about some sort of proposed deal with Iran. But in the meantime, the U.S. has launched new strikes against Iran overnight. Plus, Epstein's former assistant testifies before Congress. What she says she did not see. And a Texas teenager convicted of killing a rival track star. Why the jury rejected his claim of self-defense.
paragraphs.cn: Được rồi, các bạn hãy tiếp tục theo dõi, vì trong giờ này chúng tôi còn thảo luận về một số bang khác. Chúng tôi cũng đang chờ tin tức về một thỏa thuận đề xuất nào đó với Iran. Nhưng đồng thời, Mỹ đã tiến hành các cuộc tấn công mới vào Iran trong đêm. Ngoài ra, cựu trợ lý của Epstein đã làm chứng trước Quốc hội. Cô ấy nói rằng cô ấy không thấy gì. Và một thiếu niên Texas bị kết tội giết một ngôi sao điền kinh đối thủ. Tại sao bồi thẩm đoàn bác bỏ lời biện hộ tự vệ của anh ta.

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: It's just sad that it had to come to this. It was just a bad, bad decision he made.
paragraphs.cn: Thật đáng buồn khi mọi chuyện đi đến mức này. Anh ta đã đưa ra một quyết định rất rất tồi.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Right now, we are following the latest round of escalation between the U.S. and Iran. U.S. CENTRAL COMMAND saying they are now finished hitting targets in Iran in response to an Army helicopter getting shot down over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's state media, however, released new video overnight, saying they retaliated to the U.S. by attack -- by launching missiles against targets in Bahrain and Kuwait and an air base in Jordan.
paragraphs.cn: Hiện tại, chúng tôi đang theo dõi đợt leo thang căng thẳng mới nhất giữa Mỹ và Iran. Bộ Chỉ huy Trung tâm Mỹ cho biết họ đã hoàn thành các cuộc tấn công vào các mục tiêu ở Iran để đáp trả vụ một trực thăng Lục quân bị bắn hạ ở eo biển Hormuz. Tuy nhiên, truyền thông nhà nước Iran đã phát hành video mới vào ban đêm, tuyên bố rằng họ đã trả đũa Mỹ bằng cách phóng tên lửa vào các mục tiêu ở Bahrain, Kuwait và một căn cứ không quân ở Jordan.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Joining me now with the latest, CNN international correspondent Paula Hancocks in Abu Dhabi. Paula, can you talk about this? We know Iran, throughout the last couple of weeks, has -- they've tried to spread the war throughout the region. What have you heard from officials in Kuwait or Bahrain about confirmed damage?
paragraphs.cn: Bây giờ kết nối với phóng viên quốc tế CNN Paula Hancocks tại Abu Dhabi để cập nhật. Paula, chị có thể nói về tình hình này không? Chúng tôi biết Iran đã cố gắng lan rộng chiến tranh ra toàn khu vực trong vài tuần qua. Chị có nghe tin gì từ các quan chức Kuwait hay Bahrain về việc xác nhận thiệt hại không?

paragraphs.speaker: Paula Hancocks
paragraphs.en: The next few hours are going to be crucial as to whether or not this is an isolated escalation between the U.S. and Iran, or whether it is a step on the escalatory ladder. paragraphs.cn: We are seeing the resistance to data centers you mentioned playing out. I also think part of the challenge is that these models are improving much faster than anything we've seen before.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. Anthropic releasing a safer work mythos.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. Anthropic released a safer work myth.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Which is still scary.
paragraphs.cn: Điều này vẫn còn đáng sợ.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: After saying that they were so worried about it they only brought it to the government and certain companies.
paragraphs.cn: Sau khi bày tỏ sự lo ngại sâu sắc, họ chỉ cung cấp nó cho chính phủ và một số công ty.

paragraphs.speaker: Mills
paragraphs.en: Exactly. And because of that, I think that people haven't necessarily seen a lot of the benefits of A.I. because it's getting better so much faster. And so people are, you know, hearing about a data center in their backyard, they're hearing A.I. CEOs say that it could replace their jobs. And, by the way, you haven't really felt the positive use cases of it yet because it's getting more technical more quickly. So, I think that creates kind of a challenging soup for people. Yes.
paragraphs.cn: Đúng vậy. Chính vì thế, tôi nghĩ mọi người chưa chắc đã thấy được nhiều lợi ích của AI, bởi vì nó cải tiến quá nhanh. Vì vậy, người ta nghe nói về việc xây dựng trung tâm dữ liệu ở sân sau nhà mình, nghe CEO AI nói rằng nó có thể thay thế công việc của họ. Và, tiện thể, bạn chưa thực sự cảm nhận được những ứng dụng tích cực của nó, vì công nghệ phát triển quá nhanh. Vì vậy, tôi nghĩ điều này tạo ra một tình huống khó xử cho mọi người.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. We'll see what happens when people are ringing that bell on Wall Street. Madison Mills, thank you so much for being here. Always love to have you. And straight ahead on CNN THIS MORNING, the World Cup kicks off tomorrow. But with sky high ticket prices, problems with immigration, is this going to be the World Cup of chaos? Plus, Nancy Mace broke with President Trump over the Epstein files. Is that what costs her a shot at the governor's mansion in South Carolina, or was it something else?
paragraphs.cn: Vâng. Chúng ta hãy chờ xem điều gì sẽ xảy ra khi Phố Wall đánh cồng. Madison Mills, cảm ơn bạn rất nhiều vì đã đến. Chúng tôi luôn vui mừng khi bạn đến. Tiếp theo trong chương trình CNN This Morning, World Cup khai mạc vào ngày mai. Nhưng giá vé cao ngất ngưởng, vấn đề nhập cư tràn lan, liệu World Cup này có hỗn loạn không? Ngoài ra, Nancy Mays đã đoạn tuyệt với Tổng thống Trump vì hồ sơ Epstein. Liệu điều này có khiến bà mất cơ hội làm Thống đốc Nam Carolina, hay còn lý do khác?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, the World Cup kicks off tomorrow. I know, I said it. It kicks off across North America. But are the off the field headlines threatening to overshadow the biggest tournament in the world? So, there was a Somali referee turned away by U.S. Customs and Border Patrol in Miami when he arrived for his World Cup assignment. And he received a hero's welcome when he returned to Somalia. The Trump administration tells CNN that vetting uncovered derogatory information. They did not go into further detail.
paragraphs.cn: Vậy là World Cup khai mạc vào ngày mai. Tôi biết, tôi đã nói rồi. Nó sẽ diễn ra trên khắp Bắc Mỹ. Nhưng liệu những tin tức bên lề có đe dọa sự chú ý dành cho sự kiện thể thao lớn nhất thế giới này không? Một trọng tài người Somalia đã bị Cơ quan Hải quan và Bảo vệ Biên giới Hoa Kỳ từ chối nhập cảnh tại Miami, người đến để làm nhiệm vụ World Cup. Anh ta được chào đón như người hùng khi trở về Somalia. Chính quyền Trump nói với CNN rằng quá trình xét duyệt phát hiện thông tin bất lợi, nhưng không tiết lộ thêm chi tiết.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Now, the ref told "The New York Times," quote, "I'm just simply a referee who's trying to live his dream." Fans from Scotland said their permits were withdrawn at the final moment. Teams from Senegal, Uzbekistan were treated, quote, "like criminals," according to critics. And a player from Iraq was questioned for seven hours upon arrival in Chicago.
paragraphs.cn: Trọng tài này nói với New York Times: "Tôi chỉ là một trọng tài cố gắng thực hiện ước mơ." Các cổ động viên từ Scotland cho biết giấy phép của họ bị thu hồi vào phút cuối. Các nhà phê bình cho rằng các đội tuyển Senegal và Uzbekistan bị đối xử "như tội phạm". Một cầu thủ Iraq đã bị thẩm vấn trong bảy giờ sau khi đến Chicago.

paragraphs.speaker: Ian Wright
paragraphs.en: Every few hours it's another story. Another story about fans denied, players denied, officials denied, journalists denied. Now refs. You know something? I'm laughing, but it's not funny. It's actually not funny. Something has to be said. It's expensive tickets. Most expensive tickets ever. Know what? This is the World Cup. This is a World Cup of chaos.
paragraphs.cn: Cứ vài giờ lại có một câu chuyện mới. Lại một câu chuyện về cổ động viên bị từ chối, cầu thủ bị từ chối, quan chức bị từ chối, phóng viên bị từ chối. Bây giờ đến lượt trọng tài. Bạn biết không? Tôi cười, nhưng điều này không hề buồn cười. Thực ra chẳng buồn cười chút nào. Phải nói gì đó. Vé đắt, đắt nhất trong lịch sử. Bạn biết không? Đây là World Cup. Một kỳ World Cup hỗn loạn.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Joining us in the group chat, Dan Courtemanche. He's the executive vice president and chief communications officer for Major League Soccer. Did I say your name right? I don't want your mom being mad at me.
paragraphs.cn: Tham gia cuộc trò chuyện nhóm của chúng tôi là Dan Courtemanche, Phó Chủ tịch Điều hành kiêm Giám đốc Truyền thông của Major League Soccer. Tôi đã nói đúng tên bạn chưa? Tôi không muốn mẹ bạn giận tôi.

paragraphs.speaker: Dan Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: You did. Thank you. It's terrific to be here. Really appreciate it.
paragraphs.cn: Bạn nói đúng rồi. Cảm ơn. Rất vui được ở đây. Cảm ơn rất nhiều.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, I wanted to talk to you about this for a couple of reasons. One, your team sent over some information here, and I was noting that there is a record number of MLS players, 45, who represent countries in this, right?
paragraphs.cn: Vậy, tôi muốn nói chuyện với bạn về điều này, vì một vài lý do. Thứ nhất, nhóm của bạn đã gửi một số thông tin, và tôi để ý thấy có kỷ lục 45 cầu thủ MLS đại diện cho quốc gia của họ, đúng không?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And that you've also got -- done a lot of work through your academies to be a place to recruit people who will want to play here. Does this -- these headlines, do they kind of undermine that message that MLS has been so good, frankly, at cultivating over the last decade?
paragraphs.cn: Và các bạn cũng đã làm rất nhiều việc thông qua hệ thống đào tạo trẻ, trở thành nơi thu hút những người muốn chơi bóng ở đây. Liệu những tin tức này có làm suy yếu hình ảnh mà MLS đã xây dựng cẩn thận trong thập kỷ qua không?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Yes, I think what we're going to see is some of those headlines tomorrow when the World Cup opens in Mexico City, then certainly in North America and Canada and the United States on Friday. It's going to shift from some of those headlines, the World Cup of chaos, to celebration, to pageantry, to a party.
paragraphs.cn: Vâng, tôi nghĩ ngày mai khi World Cup khai mạc tại Thành phố Mexico, và tất nhiên vào thứ Sáu tại Bắc Mỹ, Canada và Hoa Kỳ, trọng tâm sẽ chuyển từ những tin tức về "World Cup hỗn loạn" sang lễ kỷ niệm, không khí tưng bừng và các bữa tiệc.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: But I'm saying, for the players, is this irritating, scary, frustrating? The whole point is international cooperation.
paragraphs.cn: But I want to say, for the players, is this annoying, scary, frustrating? The whole point is international cooperation.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: It's not feeling welcoming.
paragraphs.cn: It feels a bit unwelcoming.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: So, I hear you. You know, ultimately, that's a question for the players. It's not something that we've seen. And in Major League Soccer, we have players from 78 different countries. And we've been around for 31 seasons. And we haven't experienced that, challenges with visa issues or anything. But I understand where you're coming from. We want to be welcoming to all the players. We want to see them have a wonderful time here in the United States, Canada and Mexico.
paragraphs.cn: So, I hear you. You know, at the end of the day, it's the players' issue. We haven't seen that. In Major League Soccer, we have players from 78 different countries. We've been operating for 31 seasons. We haven't faced challenges like visa issues. But I understand your point. We want to welcome all players. We want to see them have a great time in the US, Canada, and Mexico.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And a lot of your incoming star players, over the last couple of years, coming to the U.S., they're coming from abroad. It's finally a place people are starting to come to and want to play, right?
paragraphs.cn: In the past few years, many upcoming star players have come to the US from abroad. It has finally become a place where people start to aspire to come and play, right?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Most definitely. The greatest of all time. Lionel Messi, by the way, scored last night in Auburn, Alabama, in a friendly against Iceland. You'll see him on Tuesday in Kansas City against Algeria. I hope he scores a lot of goals because then you can watch him playing for Inter Miami each and every weekend in Major League Soccer.
paragraphs.cn: No doubt. The greatest player of all time. By the way, Lionel Messi scored last night in a warm-up match against Iceland in Auburn, Alabama. You'll see him on Tuesday in Kansas City against Algeria. I hope he scores many goals so you can watch him play for Inter Miami every weekend in MLS.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Now, what I loved about that person who spoke at the end of our introduction is he goes, oh, I laugh because, you know, it's sort of -- sort of sad. And then he tied all of that to ticket prices. And I thought that was really fascinating, that people are also looking at this in the context of the cost of the ticket. The average get in price for a World Cup match right now, roughly $600 for the opening round of the tournament. And people are complaining about FIFA's pricing scheme.
paragraphs.cn: Now, I liked the guy who spoke at the end of our introduction. He said, oh, I laughed because, you know, it's a bit -- a bit sad. Then he linked it all to ticket prices. I found that interesting, that people are looking at it from a ticket price perspective. Currently, the average entry price for World Cup matches, group stage, is about $600. People are complaining about FIFA's pricing scheme.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And right now, if I want, I can still get a ticket to USA Paraguay. It's not sold out, and it should be.
paragraphs.cn: Now, if I wanted, I could still buy tickets for USA vs. Paraguay. The match isn't sold out yet, but it should be.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Yes, I can tell you, I'm going to be there for the U.S.- Paraguay game. I certainly anticipate it's going to be sold out. I got to tell you, there's unprecedented demand for the World Cup.
paragraphs.cn: Yes, I can tell you, I will go watch USA vs. Paraguay. I expect it will definitely sell out. I have to tell you, the demand for the World Cup is unprecedented.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Then why isn't it sold out?
paragraphs.cn: Then why isn't it sold out yet?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Much like you'll see from my Knicks tonight, where the get-in price is about $15,000 for lower level seating. Now, that said, I think you're going to see the pageantry, the celebration throughout the World Cup. But I understand where people are coming from. Also, you have to remind people that FIFA is actually a non-profit. So, all that money goes back to the 211 FIFA nations to build the sport, especially in some underdeveloped countries.
paragraphs.cn: Just like you'll see my Knicks game tonight, lower-level seats cost about $15,000. That said, I think you'll see the spectacle and celebration during the World Cup. But I understand people's thinking. Also, you have to remind people that FIFA is actually a non-profit organization. So, all the money goes back to the 211 FIFA member associations to develop football, especially in less developed countries.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK, I'm glad you brought that up because we did have a California lawmaker, Representative Sydney Kamlager-Dove. She was on the show Friday. She said something I didn't expect to hear related to ticket prices and what her concerns were. Here's what she had to say. REP. SYDNEY KAMLAGER-DOVE (D-CA): The issue is dynamic pricing. The other issue is, you buy a ticket, you don't know where that seat is going to be. You're spending over $1,000.
paragraphs.cn: Okay, glad you mentioned that, because we do have a California congresswoman, Representative Sydney Kamlager-Dove. She was on the show Friday. She said something I didn't expect to hear about ticket prices and her concerns. Here's what she said. Rep. Sydney Kamlager-Dove (D-CA): The problem is dynamic pricing. Another issue is, you buy a ticket and don't know where your seat is. You spend over $1,000.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And finally you got the -- you get the seat and you're behind a post and you can't see anything. We want to know why ticket prices are changing so dramatically from city to city. I want to know why cities aren't getting any financial support from the World Cup to actually put on the games, because World Cup is getting everything, concessions, merchandise, parking, tickets.
paragraphs.cn: Finally, you get your seat and you're behind a pillar, can't see anything. We want to know why ticket prices vary so much between cities. I want to know why cities aren't getting any financial support from the World Cup to host matches, because the World Cup takes everything: concessions, merchandise, parking, tickets.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: That was just a long list. Can you answer any of it?
paragraphs.cn: That's a lot of questions. Can you answer any of them?

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Well, I can tell you, look, we're fortunate, five of the stadiums out of the 13 markets in the United States and Canada are Major League Soccer stadiums. And I can tell you that nobody will be sitting behind posts during the World Cup games in those MLS stadiums. So, look, she brings up some valid points. She's clearly an expert on it. But we think it's going to pivot again from some of the challenges that we've seen in the media to the celebration.
paragraphs.cn: Well, I can tell you that we are lucky that 5 of the 13 stadiums in the US and Canada are MLS stadiums. I can tell you that during the World Cup, no one will be sitting behind a pillar in those MLS stadiums. She made some valid points, and she is clearly an expert in this area. But we think the focus will shift from some of the challenges the media sees to the celebration.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: And ultimately goals scored by hopefully the United States national team and they make a deep run in the tournament.
paragraphs.cn: Ultimately, hopefully the US national team will score goals and go far in the tournament.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. I think, fundamentally, nobody is arguing that it won't be good. They'll be arguing about whether or not they got to participate.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. I think basically no one will question that this will be a good thing. What they will argue about is whether they can be a part of it.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Yes. You know, and I -- you know, it's also a good reminder that FIFA actually had $60 tickets available for those passionate supporters who registered early. So, they had some affordable prices. But I certainly understand you. And for us, you also have to look at, there'll be nearly 80 fan festivals throughout the different markets. Twenty of our MLS clubs are going to have soccer celebrations. So, you're providing free accessibility to fans who may not be able to go to the games.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. You know, it also reminds us that FIFA actually offered $60 tickets for enthusiastic fans who registered early. So, they have some affordable prices. But I certainly understand you. For us, you also have to see that there will be nearly 80 fan festivals in different markets. Our 20 MLS clubs will host football celebrations. That way, those who may not be able to go to the stadium can participate for free.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK. Well, thank you, Dan, so much for being here. Good luck. I know this is your Super Bowl. That's what we say here in the U.S. instead of soccer.
paragraphs.cn: Okay. Thank you very much for coming here, Dan. Good luck. I know this is your Super Bowl -- -- that's what we say in the US, not football.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Hey, I'm a big -- I'm a University of Georgia graduate. I love gridiron football. Come on.
paragraphs.cn: Hey, I'm a die-hard -- -- I'm a University of Georgia graduate. I love American football. Really.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Oh, OK. OK. All right. Well, thank you for being here. Appreciate your time.
paragraphs.cn: Oh, okay. Okay. Well, thank you for coming here. Thank you for your time.

paragraphs.speaker: Courtemanche
paragraphs.en: Really appreciate it.
paragraphs.cn: Thank you very much.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Next on CNN THIS MORNING, Congresswoman Nancy Mace gets crushed in the South Carolina governor's race. Did going against Trump cost her the election? But first.
paragraphs.cn: Coming up next on CNN This Morning, Congresswoman Nancy Mace loses badly in the South Carolina governor's race. Did going against Trump cost her the election? But first.

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: If you're elected governor, would you continue to perform exorcisms while governor?
paragraphs.cn: If you are elected governor, will you continue to perform exorcisms during your term?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes, controversial candidate leading in the polls in Colorado. Will the strange headlines sway voters?
paragraphs.cn: Yes, a controversial candidate in Colorado is leading in the polls. Will these strange headlines affect voters?

paragraphs.speaker: Kyle Clark
paragraphs.en: Your campaign website falsely claimed that you had rescued more than 45,000 women and children. You once called in a U.S. military airstrike that killed 70 ISIS fighters. You perform exorcisms, commanding demons to come out of people. Do you understand why so many of your critics think you're a con man? VICTOR MARX (R),
paragraphs.cn: Chiến dịch tranh cử của ông đã tuyên bố sai sự thật rằng ông đã cứu hơn 45.000 phụ nữ và trẻ em. Ông đã kêu gọi không kích của quân đội Mỹ, giết chết 70 tay súng ISIS. Ông thực hiện các nghi lễ trừ tà, ra lệnh cho ma quỷ ra khỏi người. Ông có hiểu tại sao nhiều nhà phê bình cho rằng ông là kẻ lừa đảo không? Victor Marx (Đảng Cộng hòa),

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK, that was a wild ride. And it didn't stop there. That reporter in Colorado was interviewing a Republican frontrunner for governor, Victor Marx. He's a Marine Corps veteran. He runs a faith- based nonprofit. But he's made a lot of outlandish claims. And it does not end with exorcisms and rescuing women and children from slavery. He also says he killed a man when he was a child.
paragraphs.cn: Chà, đó thực sự là một cuộc trò chuyện điên rồ. Và nó vẫn chưa kết thúc. Phóng viên ở Colorado đang phỏng vấn người dẫn đầu cuộc đua tranh cử thống đốc bang của Đảng Cộng hòa, Victor Marx. Ông ấy là một cựu chiến binh Thủy quân lục chiến. Ông ấy điều hành một tổ chức phi lợi nhuận về đức tin. Nhưng ông ấy đã đưa ra nhiều tuyên bố kỳ quặc. Và không chỉ là trừ tà và giải cứu phụ nữ trẻ em khỏi cảnh nô lệ. Ông ấy còn nói rằng mình đã giết người khi còn nhỏ.

paragraphs.speaker: Kyle Clark
paragraphs.en: Your claim that your abusive stepfather forced you to kill a man when you were seven years old, is that the only person you've ever killed? VICTOR MARX (R),
paragraphs.cn: Ông tuyên bố rằng cha dượng đã ngược đãi ông buộc ông phải giết một người khi mới 7 tuổi, đó có phải là người duy nhất ông từng giết không? Victor Marx (Đảng Cộng hòa),

paragraphs.speaker: Colorado Candidate For Governor
paragraphs.en: Well, I would say, as a -- as a child, yes. But I've been in other situations where, you know, possibly people or persons died.
paragraphs.cn: Ừm, tôi muốn nói rằng, với tư cách là một đứa trẻ, thì đúng vậy. Nhưng tôi cũng đã trải qua những tình huống khác, bạn biết đấy, có thể có người hoặc một số người đã chết.

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.en: Do you think that you've killed people as an adult?
paragraphs.cn: Ông có nghĩ rằng mình đã giết người khi trưởng thành không?

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.en: Killing somebody is a pretty weighty thing. So, I'm just asking you, how many people have you killed?
paragraphs.cn: Giết người là một chuyện khá nghiêm trọng. Vì vậy, tôi chỉ hỏi ông, ông đã giết bao nhiêu người?

paragraphs.speaker: Marx
paragraphs.en: Well, if I did, I wouldn't be telling a reporter sitting here in my training center.
paragraphs.cn: Ừm, nếu tôi có giết, tôi sẽ không nói với một phóng viên đang ngồi trong trung tâm huấn luyện của tôi.

paragraphs.speaker: Clark
paragraphs.en: How about voters? Would you tell voters?
paragraphs.cn: Vậy còn cử tri thì sao? Ông có nói với cử tri không?

paragraphs.speaker: Marx
paragraphs.en: Theres no need. I don't think that's important.
paragraphs.cn: Không cần thiết. Tôi nghĩ điều đó không quan trọng.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK. Group chat is back. I wanted to talk about this because we started the hour talking about Maine, right, and Graham Platner and being like, voters, what are they thinking? You know, like, what's happening? What will these scandals matter? And then I was seeing a lot more stories about what's happening in Colorado. Just to give you a sense of where things are. In this Republican primary right now, the fundraising, Victor Marx is at $2.7 million.
paragraphs.cn: Được rồi. Nhóm chat đã quay lại. Tôi muốn nói về điều này, vì đầu giờ chúng ta đã thảo luận về Maine, đúng vậy, và Graham Platter, các cử tri đang nghĩ gì? Bạn biết đấy, chuyện gì đang xảy ra? Những vụ bê bối này sẽ có tác động gì? Và sau đó tôi thấy thêm nhiều tin tức về những gì đang xảy ra ở Colorado. Để tôi cập nhật cho bạn. Trong cuộc bầu cử sơ bộ của Đảng Cộng hòa này, hiện tại về mặt gây quỹ, Victor Marx có 2,7 triệu đô la.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: The next closest person is at around $500,000, who is a state senator. I know you're just kind of clocking into this, Jarrett, but help me understand why he's so far ahead in the money. That means people are the donors. The people with pockets are giving to him. JARRETT STEPMAN, CONTRIBUTOR, "THE DAILY SIGNAL": Yes. In fairness, that may shift a bit after this moment, seeing him not as credible candidate.
paragraphs.cn: Người đứng thứ hai có khoảng 500.000 đô la, là một thượng nghị sĩ tiểu bang. Tôi biết bạn vừa tham gia cuộc thảo luận, Jarrett, nhưng hãy giúp tôi hiểu tại sao anh ấy lại dẫn đầu về mặt tài chính như vậy. Điều đó có nghĩa là mọi người đang quyên góp. Những người giàu có đang cho anh ấy tiền. Cộng tác viên của The Daily Signal, Jarrett Stepman: Vâng, công bằng mà nói, sau khi thấy anh ấy không phải là một ứng cử viên đáng tin cậy, mọi chuyện có thể thay đổi.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman

There's a sort of a desperation saying, this guy seems like an outsider. He's, said, look, we saw this in Maine, somebody who's an outsider, in that case actually had a huge amount of success against a political establishment.
paragraphs.cn: So, it's been a long time. I think you sometimes see this in states that lean one way for a long time. Sometimes there are candidates who are not very credible. There's a sense of desperation that says, this guy looks like an outsider. paragraphs.cn: Yes. Describe how your exorcism works, when you command the demon to leave.

paragraphs.speaker: Marx
paragraphs.en: Well, that's a joke. We identify the demon, like Jesus did. We asked what their name is. And then we give them commands. And they very frequently and typically will answer through the person's mind. They'll tell us what they hear. And then we destroy them, banish them to perfect judgment from Christ.
paragraphs.cn: Well, it's a joke. We identify the demon, like Jesus did. We ask their name. Then we give them commands. They often and usually answer through the person's mind. They tell us what they heard. Then we destroy them, banishing them to the perfect judgment of Christ.

paragraphs.cn: If you are elected governor, will you continue to perform exorcisms while in office?

paragraphs.speaker: Marx

It should be alarming for all of us given this guy's track record and his history and what he just said in that interview there. We're all trying to make sense of exorcisms. And this morning about a candidate, I mean, come on. (CROSS TALK)
paragraphs.cn: He really did. Because of this and the backlash. He asked different candidates different questions, giving them factual examples. By doing so, I think he really exposed the candidates. paragraphs.cn: Yes. Yes, as a Democrat, I want to say to my Republican friends and colleagues, that's your guy. That's your candidate. They should vote for him. They should rally for him.

paragraphs.cn: They should -- -- we should (inaudible). This is their path to finally taking back Colorado. (Cross talk)

LAUREN BOEBERT (R-CO): So, I think right now in Colorado we need a motivator who can then lead. Motivation has to be key in any of our Republican races, especially in a non-election year. I believe Victor Marx will put qualified people in positions. I am ready to help him. I am not disparaging any other candidates.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I have endorsed Victor Marx, but that's not something that I've turned the volume way up on. I want candidates to be able to professionally run their own race. I do not like the hateful back and forth that I see with Republicans.
paragraphs.cn: Tôi ủng hộ Victor Marx, nhưng đó không phải điều tôi công khai ủng hộ. Tôi muốn các ứng cử viên tiến hành chiến dịch của mình một cách chuyên nghiệp. Tôi không thích những cuộc cãi vã đầy thù hận giữa những người Cộng hòa.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Today is a day of ringing endorsements, but --
paragraphs.cn: Hôm nay là một ngày đầy những sự ủng hộ mạnh mẽ, nhưng -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.en: I said it, don't (ph) turn up the volume.
paragraphs.cn: Tôi đã nói rồi, đừng (không nghe rõ) vặn to âm lượng.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish

paragraphs.cn: Không, không, đây là -- -- điều chúng ta đã thảo luận trong giờ nghỉ. Luận điểm về người đưa tin không hoàn hảo. Chúng ta cũng đã nghe điều đó về Trump, phải không?

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: You're an evangelical. Maybe you're like, I don't know about the marriages, I don't know about this, I don't know about that. But maybe he will put in people that will do the thing we want.
paragraphs.cn: Bạn là một tín đồ Phúc âm. Có thể bạn nghĩ, tôi không biết về tình trạng hôn nhân của anh ta, tôi không biết điều này, tôi không biết điều kia. Nhưng có thể anh ta sẽ bổ nhiệm những người có thể làm những gì chúng ta muốn.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.en: It's the outsider again. Look, I mean, in Maine, you had a guy who has a Nazi tattoo fighting the fascists. Was that a credible campaign? Maybe it wasn't.
paragraphs.cn: Lại là người ngoài cuộc. Ý tôi là, ở Maine, có một người xăm hình chữ thập ngoặc chiến đấu với phát xít. Đó có phải là một chiến dịch đáng tin cậy không? Có lẽ không.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Maybe he needed an exorcist.
paragraphs.cn: Có lẽ anh ta cần một người trừ tà.

paragraphs.en: And maybe he should call Colorado.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.en: But again, that's also an outsider candidate that people see as different from the establishment as it is in the state. If they think things are going the wrong way, even a charismatic, let's call it, candidate like this can punch through. Yes, the other side may say, hey, we want that guy. We want to face him. But sometimes you get what you wish for and it turns up backfiring.
paragraphs.cn: Nhưng một lần nữa, đây là một ứng cử viên ngoài cuộc, người mà mọi người cho là khác biệt so với giới tinh hoa của bang. Nếu họ nghĩ rằng mọi thứ đang đi sai hướng, ngay cả một ứng cử viên có sức hút, tạm gọi như vậy, cũng có thể nổi bật. Vâng, phía bên kia có thể nói, này, chúng tôi muốn người đó. Chúng tôi muốn đối đầu với anh ta. Nhưng đôi khi bạn nhận được thứ bạn muốn, và kết quả lại phản tác dụng.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes, that does backfire. I have one more thing about being an outsider. This one is about a member of Congress who had hoped to go into a new office in the next year. They're coming up short. Last night Republican Nancy Mace finished fifth in the GOP primary for South Carolina governor. She did not get the president's endorsement in the race. She believes it was because of her vote to release the Epstein files, which may have eventually led to her defeat. REP.
paragraphs.cn: Vâng, điều đó thực sự sẽ phản tác dụng. Tôi còn một điểm nữa về người ngoài cuộc. Điều này liên quan đến một nghị sĩ hy vọng vào một văn phòng mới vào năm tới. Họ đang tụt lại phía sau. Đêm qua, nữ nghị sĩ Cộng hòa Nancy Mace đứng thứ năm trong cuộc bầu cử sơ bộ của đảng Cộng hòa cho chức thống đốc Nam Carolina. Bà ấy không nhận được sự ủng hộ của tổng thống trong chiến dịch. Bà ấy nghĩ đó là vì bà ấy đã bỏ phiếu yêu cầu công bố hồ sơ Epstein, điều này cuối cùng có thể dẫn đến thất bại của bà.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: NANCY MACE (R-SC): I chose to expose the abusers of children, and apparently I chose wrong if the goal was winning an election. I'm at peace with that because when a candidate is OK with corruption, when a candidate is OK with cover ups, something is broken. And that's not political opinion, that is a moral emergency.
paragraphs.cn: Nancy Mace (Cộng hòa-Nam Carolina): Tôi chọn vạch trần những kẻ lạm dụng trẻ em, rõ ràng nếu mục tiêu là thắng cử, tôi đã chọn sai. Tôi cảm thấy thanh thản về điều đó, bởi vì khi một ứng cử viên dung túng tham nhũng, khi một ứng cử viên dung túng che đậy sự thật, thì có điều gì đó đã hỏng. Đó không phải là quan điểm chính trị, đó là khủng hoảng đạo đức.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Group chat is back. I'm going to just say it. There's been a lot of difficult headlines for Nancy Mace. Another person who is dealing with scandal and allegations. But at the same time, she is another person who talked about Epstein a lot, right? She's another person who got on the wrong side of Trump on certain issues. Is that reflected here, or is this really candidate quality?
paragraphs.cn: Nhóm chat đã trở lại. Tôi sẽ nói thẳng. Nancy Mace có nhiều tiêu đề khó khăn. Một người khác cũng đang đối mặt với bê bối và cáo buộc. Nhưng đồng thời, cô ấy cũng là một người thường xuyên nói về Epstein, phải không? Cô ấy đã đối đầu với Trump về một số vấn đề. Điều này có được phản ánh ở đây không, hay đây thực sự là vấn đề về chất lượng ứng cử viên?

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.en: I want to go back to something we talked about in Maine with Susan Collins, talking about the issues, and Platner as well, talking about issues to Mainers. I feel like South Carolinians didn't hear Nancy Mace talking about that. And more of those headlines and more of her getting a microphone in front of her --
paragraphs.cn: I want to go back to our discussion in Maine with Susan Collins, talking about issues, and Plattner, talking to Maine people about issues. I don't think South Carolinians hear Nancy Mace talking about those. It's more the headlines, and her getting the microphone -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.en: Or coming on this network and arguing with somebody and then saying something completely the opposite when she gets off camera, I think those things are what really caught up to her in this race.
paragraphs.cn: Or coming on this network to argue with someone, then saying the exact opposite after stepping down. I think that's what really gets her attention in this campaign.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I also heard she didn't do ad spend on
paragraphs.cn: I also heard she hasn't spent money on ads -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Like only digital. Is that wise?
paragraphs.cn: Like only doing digital ads. Is that wise?

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.en: Well, I -- first of all, Donald Trump still has a stranglehold on the Republican primary. And that is why his party is getting ripped apart.
paragraphs.cn: Well, I -- -- first, Donald Trump still firmly controls the Republican primary. That's why his party is splitting.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.en: And didn't endorse her.
paragraphs.cn: And not supporting her.

paragraphs.speaker: Rose
paragraphs.en: Because the primary base is so extreme and they are so disassociated at this point from independents and moderates nationwide. But Nancy Mace took it one step further. She decided to antagonize, not just Donald Trump, but literally everyone in her life to include just random folks at the airport. So, what you saw here was -- and politicians do this sometimes. They jump into races not to win, but to bolster their public profile for something they're after.
paragraphs.cn: Because the primary base voters are very extreme, and they are now severely out of touch with independents and moderates nationwide. But Nancy Mace goes further. She decided to fight, not just Donald Trump, but almost everyone in her life, including random people at the airport. So, what you see here is -- -- politicians sometimes do this. They don't run to win, but to boost their public profile for some goal.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. Well, this is the kind of thing that is in my group chat because every member of the YOLO caucus, the people who limp back to Congress from their primaries, they're really upset. And they plan to spend the next couple of months antagonizing the White House. Do you think Nancy Mace will be one of those?
paragraphs.cn: Yes. Well, that's exactly what's in my group chat, because every member of the YOLO caucus, those who barely made it back to Congress from the primaries, are very unhappy. They plan to confront the White House in the coming months. Do you think Nancy Mace will be one of them?

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.en: I think she's going to in her final months. I mean she's already burned all the bridges. She's -- I think the voters in her state didn't like the drama and they said, hey, we don't want that actually running the place.
paragraphs.cn: I think she will do it in the final months. I mean, she has already burned all bridges. She -- -- I don't think voters in her state like this drama. They say, hey, we don't want someone like that actually running the place.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. That's a good point, you're not effective if you're at odds with everyone.
paragraphs.cn: Yes, well said. If you're at odds with everyone, you won't be effective.

paragraphs.speaker: Stepman
paragraphs.en: You're not effective. And you're infective (ph). And she's going to continue this. Obviously, if she's done it up to this point, I don't think she's going to stop. But I -- she's already burned the bridges with President Trump, clearly. So, what have you got to lose now?
paragraphs.cn: You won't be effective, and you'll be contagious (sound). She'll keep going. Obviously, if she's done it this far, I don't think she'll stop. But I -- -- she's completely fallen out with President Trump, that's clear. So, what do you have to lose now?

paragraphs.en: And they're giving tickets to combat (INAUDIBLE).

paragraphs.cn: 没错。而且 -- -- 正是如此。特朗普一直把它当作一个工具，用于他与泰森的关系以及他在特朗普泰姬陵或其他地方举办的比赛。

paragraphs.speaker: Leon
paragraphs.en: He would always be in this space. He's always had these relationships because those are the kind of voters that go out and vote for him. That's the thing that we've always talked about.
paragraphs.cn: 他总是在这个领域。他一直有这些关系，因为那些是出去投票给他的选民。这是我们一直谈论的事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: All right, my friend at "The Daily Signal," what's in your group chat?

paragraphs.en: So, I'm going to bring up the Knicks because it's a big thing. I moved here to New York --
paragraphs.cn: 那么，我要提一下尼克斯队，因为这是件大事。我搬到纽约来 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Why do I even bother?

paragraphs.en: I know. Why did you even bother.

paragraphs.speaker: Leon

It may not come again. So, you know what --
paragraphs.cn: 你知道吗，这对我很重要。我来自加州奥克兰，美国最悲伤的体育城市。我们失去了所有的运动队。所以，就凭尼克斯队 -- -- 你看，我有很多纽约体育迷朋友，他们这辈子几乎没见过尼克斯队、喷气机队或其他队伍有什么作为，而现在他们能和孩子一起享受这一刻。这机会可能不会再有了。所以，你知道吗 -- --

paragraphs.en: I love it. I love it. It's communal.

paragraphs.en: It's communal. Its wonderful.
